title: in, on, or at? (time and place)
lesson: Use 'at' for precise points — clock times and exact places (at 9 a.m., at the door); 'on' for days, dates, and surfaces (on Monday, on the table); and 'in' for longer periods and enclosed spaces (in May, in the room). Note 'in the morning' but 'at night'.

items.explanation: Days take 'on' (on Monday); clock times take 'at' (at 9 a.m.).
'At' is used for precise points in time: at midnight, at noon, at 3 o'clock.

items.text: She arrived {1} the hotel {2} Friday {3} the afternoon.
items.explanation: 'At' for a specific place seen as a point (at the hotel); 'on' for days (on Friday); 'in' for parts of the day (in the afternoon).

items.text: The keys are {1} the table {2} the kitchen.
items.explanation: Surfaces take 'on' (on the table); enclosed spaces take 'in' (in the kitchen).

items.text: There's a good film {1} TV tonight.
items.explanation: 'On TV' is a fixed phrase — something is broadcast on television.
Years and months both take 'in' (in 1990, in July).

items.text: They met {1} the park {2} a cold day {3} winter.

blanks.hint: an open area you can be inside

items.explanation: 'In the park' (area); 'on a cold day' (a specific day); 'in winter' (seasons take 'in').

items.text: I usually wake up early {1} the morning, but I sleep late {2} night.
items.explanation: Parts of the day take 'in' (in the morning), but 'night' is an exception: at night.

items.text: My grandmother lives {1} a small village {2} the coast.
items.explanation: Towns and villages take 'in' (in a village); 'on the coast' uses 'on' for a line or edge.

items.text: The shop closes {1} the weekend and reopens {2} Monday.
items.explanation: 'At the weekend' is the standard British phrase; days of the week take 'on' (on Monday).

items.text: There is a beautiful picture {1} the wall {2} my bedroom.
items.explanation: A picture sits on a surface (on the wall), inside an enclosed space (in my bedroom).
